Name: tensorefficientinference
Title: Tensor-Core Efficient Inference for Long-Context Language Models
Short Hypothesis: By optimizing the utilization of Tensor Cores for dequantization and memory management in low-bit KV cache, we can significantly reduce the latency of long-context language model inference without compromising accuracy.
Related Work: Existing works like BitDecoding (Du et al., 2025) have shown the potential of using low-bit KV cache and Tensor Cores to speed up long-context LLM inference. However, they primarily focus on specific GPU architectures and do not provide a generalized framework that can be applied across different hardware. Our proposal aims to extend and generalize these techniques to a wider range of GPUs and to further optimize the dequantization process.
Abstract: Long-context Large Language Models (LLMs) require significant memory and bandwidth during autoregressive decoding, leading to high inference latency. While low-bit KV cache quantization can reduce memory footprint, existing systems often underutilize Tensor Cores, leading to suboptimal performance. This proposal introduces TensorEfficientInference, a framework that optimizes the use of Tensor Cores for dequantization and memory management in low-bit KV cache. By leveraging hardware-specific optimizations and novel warp-level parallelization strategies, TensorEfficientInference aims to significantly reduce decoding latency while maintaining accuracy. We will evaluate our framework on a range of GPU architectures, including Hopper, Ada, and Ampere, and demonstrate substantial improvements in latency and throughput.
Experiments: Develop a baseline system using existing low-bit KV cache techniques and Tensor Core dequantization methods.
Implement hardware-specific optimizations for dequantization, focusing on warp-level parallelization and efficient memory access patterns.
Evaluate the performance of TensorEfficientInference on different GPU architectures (Hopper, Ada, Ampere) using a variety of long-context LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-3.1-8B).
Compare the latency, throughput, and energy efficiency of TensorEfficientInference against state-of-the-art systems like BitDecoding and QServe.
Conduct A/B latency races under fixed accuracy budgets, reporting p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.

Instrument the system to collect detailed profiling metrics, including occupancy, achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity, to attribute performance gains to specific hardware optimizations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Compatibility issues across different GPU architectures may arise, requiring additional hardware-specific tuning.
The complexity of hardware-level optimizations may introduce bugs that are difficult to diagnose and fix.
The performance gains may vary across different models and contexts, necessitating extensive benchmarking and fine-tuning.
The overhead of hardware instrumentation and profiling may impact real-time performance, although this can be mitigated with careful design.
